gene_description: Dopamine Receptor D4 — G-protein coupled receptor highly expressed in the prefrontal cortex, implicated in ADHD susceptibility and modulating stimulant medication response.

source.title: Pharmacogenetics of methylphenidate in childhood attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: long-term effects
source.finding: DRD4 rs1800955 -521C/T promoter variant influenced long-term methylphenidate efficacy in ADHD children, with the T allele associated with altered DRD4 expression and variable stimulant response.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele at the DRD4 -521 promoter position. This variant increases DRD4 transcription, altering dopaminergic signaling in the prefrontal cortex. Associated with variable long-term methylphenidate response in ADHD, potentially requiring more careful dose titration. DRD4 variants also contribute to ADHD susceptibility itself.
TT.recommendation: Initiate stimulant therapy at standard dose but monitor response carefully over the first 4-8 weeks. If methylphenidate response is inconsistent, consider amphetamine-based alternatives or combination with behavioral therapy. Long-term follow-up recommended.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. One copy of the T allele with intermediate DRD4 promoter activity. Moderate influence on stimulant medication response with generally adequate treatment outcomes.
TC.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing is appropriate. Monitor treatment response during initial titration and long-term maintenance.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. One copy of the T allele with intermediate DRD4 promoter activity. Moderate influence on stimulant medication response with generally adequate treatment outcomes.
CT.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing is appropriate. Monitor treatment response during initial titration and long-term maintenance.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the reference C allele. Normal DRD4 promoter activity and standard dopaminergic signaling. Expected typical response to stimulant medications.
CC.recommendation: Standard ADHD stimulant dosing per clinical guidelines. No pharmacogenomic adjustment needed.
